projects.name: Solar + Energy Storage on Garage
projects.description: Install 5-7 solar panels on south-facing garage roof face (20x12 ft, ~2-2.8 kW) plus battery storage. Solar = standalone ROI investment (~5yr payback at $0.42/kWh). Battery = generator alternative (no lot space for generator). 30% federal ITC applies to both when paired.
projects.notes: DESIGN: South garage face only. 5 portrait panels = 2.0 kW; 7 mixed = 2.8 kW. ~3,200-4,500 kWh/yr production (20-26% offset). All-in after ITC: solar ~$9-10k, battery ~$9-10k. VENDORS: Sunergy (sunergy.com) — EnergySage 2026 CA Installer of Year, Enphase Platinum, no door-to-door. NRG Clean Power (nrgcleanpower.com) — Diamond Certified, 35yr, San Mateo County, 40yr warranty. Tesla Energy (tesla.com/energy) — online only. AVOID: Sunrun.

tasks.title: Analyze energy usage and size system
tasks.notes: 17,178 kWh/yr at $0.42/kWh avg. Target: 5-7 panels south garage face (20x12 ft) = 2-2.8 kW. ~5yr payback after ITC.

tasks.title: Research and shortlist solar installers
tasks.notes: Shortlist: Sunergy (sunergy.com, Enphase Platinum, no door-to-door), NRG Clean Power (nrgcleanpower.com, Diamond Certified, San Mateo County), Tesla Energy (tesla.com/energy, online only). Avoid Sunrun.

tasks.title: Contact installers for quotes
tasks.notes: Spec: south garage face (20x12 ft, 5-7 panels, ~2-2.8 kW), paired battery (Enphase IQ or Powerwall 3), detached garage sub-panel interconnect. Ask each for: $/W, panel brand, inverter type, battery spec, warranty, timeline, ITC total. Request free shade/LIDAR analysis.

tasks.title: Compare quotes and select installer
tasks.notes: Prefer Enphase microinverters over string inverter. Compare $/W, battery spec, warranty, timeline. 30% ITC on full system incl. battery.

tasks.title: Confirm ITC timing — credit files in year PTO is granted
tasks.notes: 30% federal ITC on Form 5695. Claimed in tax year Permission to Operate granted by PG&E. Battery qualifies only when solar-paired. Plan install timing accordingly.

insights.summary: The home consistently reports 9–10 connected mobile devices, which is unusually high for a four-person household and, combined with zero camera motion events, strongly suggests a network configuration issue misclassifying IoT devices as mobile or a previously connected device lingering. This warrants a quick network check to rule out unauthorized access or configuration drift.

The sustained high presence count (8–11 devices) combined with zero camera activity on a Thursday afternoon strongly suggests the household is home all day with minimal movement, likely indicating a day off, remote work schedule, or family gathering rather than a typical weekday routine.
The presence count jumping to 10 devices at 1:02 PM on a Thursday strongly correlates with typical school dismissal times, indicating Miller and Penelope have likely returned home.

The presence count suddenly jumped from 8 to 10 devices in one minute (13:01-13:02) after hours of stability, indicating a coordinated arrival or network connection event. Since no cameras triggered, these new devices likely entered through a non-monitored entry like the garage, so verifying who is home is recommended.

The presence count stabilized at 8 devices home by 10:07 AM, which is unusually high for a Thursday mid-morning with two school-age children. Combined with zero camera activity, this suggests the family is likely still home rather than having departed, indicating routine automations may need adjustment.

The steady decline in connected mobile devices (11 to 7) aligns with a typical Thursday morning departure, but the complete absence of camera activity during this window is unusual. This gap makes it impossible to confirm physical departure versus a camera outage or devices going offline, warranting a quick system check.

The rapid 9–11 device fluctuation at 4:49 AM with zero camera or light activity is a known UniFi presence detection artifact counting IoT devices or Wi-Fi probe noise rather than actual human movement. This is a low-priority sensor quirk, not a household activity indicator.

Mobile device count increased from 8 to 10 at 17:39, aligning with typical after-school arrival times and indicating a normal late afternoon household transition. Roxanne’s sleep tracker also logged a 9.12-hour session at 16:08, which may be a tracking sync artifact or an unusually long rest worth monitoring if fatigue is observed.

The device count dropping to 7 around 15:26 and rising back to 8-9 by 16:00 aligns with the children returning from school; the complete lack of camera activity during this window suggests they are likely in bedrooms or study areas outside camera coverage, which is typical for this age group after school.

Mobile device presence consistently shows 7-9 devices home on a Wednesday afternoon, exceeding the expected count for a typical school day. Combined with Roxanne’s extended sleep report at 16:08, this suggests unexpected household occupancy (e.g., kids home early or guests), warranting a quick status check.
